skill.name: firebase-auth-basics
skill.description: Guide for setting up and using Firebase Authentication. Use this skill when the user's app requires user sign-in, user management, or secure data access using auth rules.
skill.summary: Documentation for implementing Firebase Authentication with support for email/password, federated providers, and security rules.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: This skill consists entirely of Markdown documentation files teaching Firebase Authentication implementation. Static analyzer flagged code fence markers in documentation as 'external commands' and reference URLs as 'network' patterns - all false positives. No executable code present. The skill provides legitimate instructional content for Firebase Auth SDK usage.

content.value_statement: Add secure user authentication to your application without building auth infrastructure from scratch. This skill guides you through Firebase Authentication setup with support for multiple sign-in providers and security rules.
actual_capabilities: Guide setup of Firebase Authentication in web applications
Provide code examples for email/password authentication
Demonstrate federated sign-in with Google, Facebook, Apple, Twitter, GitHub, Microsoft, Yahoo
Explain anonymous and email link authentication methods
Document security rules using request.auth for data protection
Show Firebase CLI commands for auth provisioning
limitations: Documentation-focused; does not execute Firebase commands directly
Web SDK examples only; mobile SDK patterns differ
Some providers require Firebase Console configuration outside CLI scope
Does not cover advanced topics like multi-factor authentication or custom claims

use_cases.title: New Application Authentication
use_cases.description: Add user sign-in to a new web application with minimal setup time.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developer building a SaaS product

use_cases.title: Federated Identity Integration
use_cases.description: Enable users to sign in with existing Google, Apple, or social media accounts.
use_cases.target_user: Startup CTO prioritizing user conversion rates

use_cases.title: Secure Data Access Patterns
use_cases.description: Implement Firestore or Storage security rules based on user authentication state.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineer designing multi-tenant data access

prompt_templates.title: Basic Email Authentication Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up email/password authentication for my web app using Firebase Auth. Show me how to create a new user account and handle sign-in errors.

prompt_templates.title: Google Sign-In Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: I want users to sign in with their Google accounts. Show me the Firebase Auth code for popup-based Google sign-in and how to access the user's email and profile picture.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding social login to increase sign-up conversion

prompt_templates.title: Security Rules for User Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Write Firestore security rules that ensure users can only read and write their own user document. The collection is 'users' and each document ID should match the user's UID.
prompt_templates.scenario: Protecting user data with auth-based rules

prompt_templates.title: Email Link Authentication Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement passwordless sign-in using email links. Show me how to send the sign-in email and handle the callback to complete authentication on the landing page.

output_examples.input: Set up Google sign-in for my Firebase web app
output: Call signInWithPopup(auth, provider) to trigger the Google sign-in flow
Handle the result to access user info and Google access token
Implement error handling for cancelled sign-ins and account conflicts

output_examples.input: Write a security rule to protect user documents
output: allow read, write: if request.auth != null && request.auth.uid == userId;

best_practices: Use onAuthStateChanged listener to react to authentication state changes throughout your app lifecycle
Store sensitive data only in Firestore/Storage with proper security rules, not in client-side storage
Enable email verification for email/password accounts to reduce fake signups
Use Firebase Console to configure OAuth providers with proper authorized domains
anti_patterns: Storing authentication tokens in localStorage - Firebase Auth handles token refresh automatically
Checking authentication only at page load - use auth state listeners for real-time updates
Writing security rules that trust client-provided data instead of verifying with request.auth
Skipping error handling on auth operations - always catch and display auth errors to users

faq.question: Do I need the Firebase CLI to use this skill?
faq.answer: No, the Firebase CLI is optional. You can configure authentication providers entirely through the Firebase Console. The CLI provides an alternative for project setup and local emulator usage.

faq.question: Which authentication provider should I choose for my app?
faq.answer: Google Sign-In is recommended as a secure default with high user familiarity. Email/password provides maximum compatibility but requires email verification. Consider offering multiple providers to maximize sign-up conversion.

faq.question: Are Firebase Authentication tokens secure?
faq.answer: Yes, Firebase uses industry-standard JWT tokens that are automatically refreshed. The SDK handles token management securely. Never manually extract or store tokens - let Firebase Auth manage them.

faq.question: Can I use Firebase Auth with my existing backend?
faq.answer: Yes, Firebase Auth integrates with custom backends. Use the ID token from the client to verify user identity on your backend using the Firebase Admin SDK.

faq.question: What happens to anonymous users if they want to upgrade to a permanent account?
faq.answer: Firebase supports account linking. Sign in the anonymous user first, then link a permanent credential (email/password or OAuth provider) to preserve their data and anonymous UID.

faq.question: Do security rules work with all Firebase services?
faq.answer: Security rules are available for Firestore, Realtime Database, and Cloud Storage. Each service has its own rule syntax but all support request.auth for authentication checks.
